coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of carbon dots@restricted access molecularly imprinted polymers for selective detection of metronidazole in serum
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Haochi
Ding, Jie
Zhang, Kun
Ding, Lan
coredata.dc:description: A custom-tailored design was proposed for the fabrication of carbon dots coupled with restricted access materials and molecularly imprinted polymers (CDs@RAM-MIPs) to detect metronidazole (MNZ). Biomass carbon dots (CDs) were derived from longan peels assisted with high pressure microwave, and had the merits of eco-friendly, excellent photostability and low toxicity. In this work, glycidyl methacrylate was used as a co-polymeric monomer to increase hydroxyl groups on the surface of synthetic materials, which eliminated the interference of biological macromolecules. The specific binding cavities of CDs@RAM-MIPs were formed after removing the template molecule (MNZ). The obtained CDs@RAM-MIPs can selectively capture MNZ through the specific interaction between recognition sites and MNZ, and obey photoinduced electron transfer fluorescence quenching mechanism. The highly sensitive and selective fluorescent sensor based CDs@RAM-MIPs had a wide linear range (50–1200 ng mL−1) and a low detection limit (17.4 ng mL−1) for MNZ. It has been utilized to detect MNZ in serum with recoveries of 93.5%–102.7%, and the relative standards (RSDs) were 1.9%–3.6%, respectively. This work provides a thoughtful strategy for preparation and application of CDs@RAM-MIPs, which presages its great potential for detecting trace compounds in real samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biomass carbon dots
Restricted access
Molecularly imprinted polymers
Fluorescent probe
Metronidazole

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Factors affecting the optical properties of CDs
Factors affecting the optical properties of CDs@RAM-MIPs
Mechanism of CDs@RAM-MIPs for MNZ detection
Specificity and selectivity for MNZ detection
Practical application in analyzing serum sample

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of biomass CDs synthesis(A), CDs@RAM-MIPs (B) and recognition process of MNZ in serum sample (C).
TEM images of CDs (A) and CDs@RAM-MIPs (B), XRD pattern of CDs@RAM-MIPs (C) and FT-IR spectra of CDs, CDs@MIPs, CDs@RAM-MIPs (D).
XPS full survey, high-resolution XPS of C 1s, O 1s and N 1s spectra of biomass CDs (A) and CDs@RAM-MIPs (B).

Fluorescence quenching of CDs@RAM-MIPs in different reaction times (A). The effect of pH on fluorescent intensity of CDs@RAM-MIPs (F0) and on the fluorescence quenching of CDs@RAM-MIPs-MNZ (F) (B). The existing form and distribution curve of MNZ under different pH (C). The effect of irradiation time to CDs@RAM-MIPs (D).

The static state contact angles and water drop profiles in CDs@MIPs and CDs@RAM-MIPs (A). The photographs of CDs@MIPs (left), CDs@RAM-MIPs (right) after being settled down for 0 min (a), 10 min (b), 20 min (c), and 30 min (d) (B).
UV spectra of MNZ (line I), CDs@RAM-MIPs with MNZ (line II), CDs@RAM-MIPs (line III), and fluorescence spectra of CDs@RAM-MIPs (line IV) (A). Schematic of fluorescence quenching mechanism (B).

Fluorescence spectra of CDs@RAM-MIPs with MNZ (A, inset: Linear relationship between quenching efficiency (F0/F) of CDs@RAM-MIPs and MNZ concentration) and CDs@RAM-NIPs with MNZ (B, inset: Linear relationship between quenching efficiency (F0/F) of CDs@RAM-NIPs and MNZ concentration).
Protein exclusion test for BSA by microspheres.
Comparison of different methods to detect MNZ in different samples.

row.entry: SPE with a stable-bonded cyano column
Carbon paste electrode modified with duplex MIP hybrid film

head.ce:title: Fabrication of carbon dots@restricted access molecularly imprinted polymers for selective detection of metronidazole in serum

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Jilin University, 2699 Qianjin Street, Changchun, 130012, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Jilin University, 2699 Qianjin Street, Changchun 130012, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Urban Water Resource and Environment, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin, 150090, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Urban Water Resource and Environment

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Urban Water Resource and Environment, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin, 150090, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shandong Institute of Non-metallic Materials, Jinan, 250031, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shandong Institute of Non-metallic Materials, Jinan, 250031, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A custom-tailored design was proposed for the fabrication of carbon dots coupled with restricted access materials and molecularly imprinted polymers (CDs@RAM-MIPs) to detect metronidazole (MNZ). Biomass carbon dots (CDs) were derived from longan peels assisted with high pressure microwave, and had the merits of eco-friendly, excellent photostability and low toxicity. In this work, glycidyl methacrylate was used as a co-polymeric monomer to increase hydroxyl groups on the surface of synthetic materials, which eliminated the interference of biological macromolecules. The specific binding cavities of CDs@RAM-MIPs were formed after removing the template molecule (MNZ). The obtained CDs@RAM-MIPs can selectively capture MNZ through the specific interaction between recognition sites and MNZ, and obey photoinduced electron transfer fluorescence quenching mechanism. The highly sensitive and selective fluorescent sensor based CDs@RAM-MIPs had a wide linear range (50–1200 ng mL−1) and a low detection limit (17.4 ng mL−1) for MNZ. It has been utilized to detect MNZ in serum with recoveries of 93.5%–102.7%, and the relative standards (RSDs) were 1.9%–3.6%, respectively. This work provides a thoughtful strategy for preparation and application of CDs@RAM-MIPs, which presages its great potential for detecting trace compounds in real samples.

Schematic diagram of the preparation process of CDs@RAM-MIPs.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Metronidazole (MNZ) was used as antiprotozoa and antibacterial medicine [1]. Based on the previous study, MNZ was proved to cause animals cancer [2]. Therefore, the detection of MNZ with a sensitive and selective approach is significant for human and animal health. To date, various techniques including capillary electrophoresis [3], high-performance liquid chromatography [4], and electrochemical techniques [5] have been applied to detect MNZ. Most approaches have some disadvantages, such as tedious sample preparation process, time-consumption or expensive instruments, which limit their applications. In comparison with other techniques, fluorescence techniques for the detection of MNZ have aroused much attention owing to the advantages of simple, high sensitivity and time saving.

Since its accidental discovery [6], carbon dots (CDs) have become an emerging material. Compared with metal semiconductor quantum dots (QDs), CDs have lower cytotoxicity and tunable emissions [7]. According to the carbon source classification, there are three main methods for preparing CDs, containing thermal condensation then carbonization of small organic molecules [8], acidizing carbon materials [9], and natural biological resources [10]. The main limitations of acidizing carbon materials method are tedious process and the use of strong acid. The other two methods can be used to prepare CDs in one pot without additional surface functionalization. However, biomass materials are more abundant than that of small molecules used. So far, various carbon precursors have been used to synthesize CDs for heavy metals detection and environmental analysis [11,12]. However, the fluorescent sensor is susceptible to the influencing factors of environmental, sample concentration and detection equipment, and has problems such as weak anti-interference ability, poor selectivity, and low sensitivity. Therefore, it is a challenge to realize the selective identification of target analytes in complex systems and improve the anti-interference ability of the method.

Molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) are ideal candidates to improve the selectivity of fluorescence detection. Molecular recognition sites in synthetic MIPs can specifically bind target molecules [13,14]. Some fluorescent probes based MIPs and QDs have been proposed including Mn-doped ZnS QDs for cyphenothrin detection [15], and graphene QDs embedded MIPs for the determination of paranitrophenol [16]. In our previous research [17], we prepared a novel MIP-coated CDs by hydrothermal synthesis for the detection of oxytetracycline in honey. However, there are some problems, such as long hydrothermal time for CDs preparation and the possible interference of biological macromolecules on the analyzing of complex samples. With the in-depth study of MIPs, restricted access materials (RAMs) have aroused wide attention due to their superior properties. RAMs are able to inject biological samples into analytical systems without pretreatment. RAMs usually provide dual surface configurations [18]. The inner layer can only allow small molecules to enter and has the ability for their separation and retention. The outer surface utilizes hydrophilic interactions and size exclusion to prevent biological macromolecules from entering the inner layer. Recently, RAMs have been coupled with MIPs to directly analyze plasma [19,20] and serum [21,22] samples.

Herein, we proposed a specific strategy to prepare multifunctional composites by a simple polymerization method. Longan peels were used to produce biomass CDs through high pressure microwave, which can decrease preparation time and toxic solvents consumption. In the synthesis process of CDs@RAM-MIPs, CDs, MNZ, acrylamide (AM), and ethyl glycoldimethacrylate (EGDMA) were acted as fluorescent materials, template molecules, functional monomers and cross-linker, respectively. Glycidyl methacrylate (GMA) contains active epoxy groups which can be used as the co-polymerization functional monomers. Subsequently, the epoxy groups can hydrolyze to produce a large amount of hydroxyl groups, which makes the materials hydrophilic and eliminates the interference of biological macromolecules. Furthermore, the hydrophilic characteristic, optical and analysis performance of CDs@RAM-MIPs were studied. The results indicated that CDs@RAM-MIPs as specific probes had high selectivity to detect trace MNZ in serum samples.

Perchloric acid (98%), MNZ (99%), bovine serum albumin (96%, BSA), quinine sulfate (99%), chloramphenicol (98%), ornidazole (98%, ONZ), and dimetridazole (97%, DMZ) were acquired from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). AM (99%), EGDMA (98%), GMA (97%), and 2,2′-azobis (2-methylpropionitrile) (99%, AIBN) were purchased from Kermel (Tianjin, China). Longan was purchased from local markets in Changchun. Phosphate buffer solution (PBS) of different pH was obtained by a certain amount of 0.2 M Na2HPO4·12H2O (99%), 0.2 M NaH2PO4·2H2O (99%) and high purity water. All reagents were of analytical grade and used without further treatment.

The morphological of CDs and CDs@RAM-MIPs was characterized by a transmission electron microscopy (TEM, H-7650, Hitachi, Japan). The XRD pattern of CDs@RAM-MIPs was recorded on an XRD-6100 spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). An electron spectrometer (ESCALAB250, Thermo Electron, USA) was used to perform the elemental analysis. FT-IR spectra were collected by a Nicolet FT-IR360 spectrometer (Madison, USA). The contact angles were collected by a contact angle measurement instrument (CA, OCA20, Germany). The UV spectra and fluorescence spectra were investigated using a TU-1901 spectrometer (PERSEE, China) and an F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan).

Biomass CDs were obtained through a high pressure microwave approach. The longan peels (2 g) were mixed with 30 mL of water, and then added into a microwave digestion tank. Microwave irradiation was proceeded through a programmed heating process: start from room temperature to 200 °C, holding at 200 °C for 30 min. The obtained solution was filtered by a 0.22 μm membrane. The quantum yield (QY) of biomass CDs was obtained through the following equation (1).
(1)
Q
C D
=
Q
R
•
I
C D
I
R
•
A
R
A
C D
•
η
C D
2
η
R
2
where I, A and η represent the luminescent intensity, absorbance at excitation wavelength, the refractive index of solvent, respectively. Quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 (QY = 0.54) is used as the reference. The subscript R represents the standard substance [23].

The CDs@MIPs microspheres were synthesized as follows: Firstly, 0.5 mmol of MNZ was dispersed in ethanol (150 mL) to make MNZ completely dissolve. After dissolution, AM (4 mmol), EGDMA (10 mmol), AIBN (0.6 mmol), GMA (400 μL) and CDs (0.1 g) were added into the reaction system under N2 atmosphere with stirring at 60 °C for 24 h.

CDs@MIPs microspheres (400 mg) were added to 10% perchloric acid (25 mL), and then stirred at room temperature for 24 h. The synthesized CDs@RAM-MIPs composites were washed by high-purity water three times, then dried at 60 °C. The polymers were eluted with a mixture of methanol and acetic acid (95:5, v/v), until MNZ was completely removed. CDs@RAM-non-imprinted polymers (CDs@RAM-NIPs) microspheres were synthesized by the identical preparation process without MNZ.

The parameters of fluorescent measurement including scan speed (240 nm min−1), excitation voltage (750 V) and slit widths (10 nm) were installed. A series of MNZ concentrations were mixed with 0.5 mg mL−1 CDs@RAM-MIPs (1:1, v/v) and adjusted to pH = 8 by PBS buffer. After 5 min of fully mixing, the fluorescent signal was measured with excitation at 360 nm.

Firstly, 1.0 mL of equine serum was combined with acetonitrile (1.0 mL) to precipitate proteins. The mixture was shaken for 10 min and centrifuged (7000 rpm, 10 min) to obtain the supernatant, which was filtered by a 0.22 μm membrane. Then, the filtrates were diluted five times with PBS buffer (pH = 8). Then the sample was mixed with 0.5 mg mL−1 CDs@RAM-MIPs (1:1, v/v) and incubated for 5 min. The MNZ content in serum sample was calculated by the variation of CDs@RAM-MIPs fluorescence intensity.

In this experiment, biomass CDs derived from longan peels were synthesized in one pot under high pressure microwave within 30 min (Fig. 1 A). The preparation of CDs has several notable advantages. Firstly, longan is rich in varieties and affordable in price. The longan peels are the waste of longan after being eaten, 500 g of longan costs 1.5 dollars on average. Secondly, the synthesis process is green, simple and scalable to large volumes. Thirdly, the production yield is relatively high. Raw materials (10 g) can at least prepare 2.3 g of CDs, and the yield was about 23% (w/w) which is much higher than CDs derived from food waste (1.2%) [24], and coffee grounds (12%–15%) [25].

In addition, high pressure microwave technique was a simple and efficient way to prepare CDs. During the reaction, water was used as reaction medium, and boiling water vapor can produce bubbles to mix the reactants well and promote uniform nucleation growth. In comparison with conventional heating, a remarkable increase in reaction rate was achieved [26]. Moreover, high pressure microwave technique can provide uniform, efficient and strong energy, which greatly reduces the reaction time for the preparation of CDs.

We also investigated the effect of reaction temperatures (140 °C, 160 °C, 180 °C, 200 °C, 220 °C) on the preparation of CDs. The CDs fluorescence intensity raised continuously when reaction temperature was increased. We speculate that low temperatures are not enough for surface functionalization and carbonization [27]. However, high temperatures may exceed the tolerance of microwave digestion tank and increase the dangerous. Thus, 200 °C was chosen. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity increased as the microwave radiation time (0–30 min) was increased. When the microwave radiation time was longer than 30 min, the increase of fluorescence intensity became gentle. Thus, the synthesis conditions of CDs were optimized to be heated at 200 °C for 30 min.

Fig. 1B shows the main preparation process of CDs@RAM-MIPs. The CDs@RAM-MIPs composites were prepared by polymerization reaction using biomass CDs as fluorescent materials, MNZ as templates, AM as functional monomers, EGDMA as crosslinker and AIBN as initiator. Moreover, GMA is used as co-monomers in molecularly imprinted system. It contains epoxy rings, which can less interfere with the polymerization reaction [28]. The epoxy rings were then opened with diluted perchloric acid to provide hydrophilic modification on the surface of CDs@RAM-MIPs, which can prevent or reduce the adhesion of macromolecules [29]. The results indicated that CDs@RAM-MIPs can achieve the ideal protein exclusion effect and improve the selective recognition ability (Fig. 1C).

In the preparation of CDs@RAM-MIPs, MNZ acting as template molecules can interact with functional monomers through hydrogen bonds, which affects recognition sites formation on the surface of CDs@RAM-MIPs. Thus, the amount of MNZ was also investigated. Different CDs@RAM-MIPs composites were synthesized by changing the amount of MNZ (0.1 mmol, 0.3 mmol, 0.5 mmol, 0.8 mmol, and 1.0 mmol). The highest imprinting factor (IF, KsvCDs@RAM-MIPs/KsvCDs@RAM-NIPs) was achieved when the amount of MNZ was 0.5 mmol.

In addition, the fluorescent labeled molecularly imprinted sensor (CDs@RAM-MIPs) has been successfully applied to detect MNZ. As MNZ does not exist in the system, the electron-hole recombination can result in the fluorescence of CDs@RAM-MIPs. MNZ was used as electron acceptor and prevented the recombination of holes and electrons at CDs interface, leading to fluorescence quenching.

The characteristic UV peak of biomass CDs was located at 278 nm (Fig. S1A, line I), which was ascribed to the n-π* transition of CO, NH2, COOH bonds and the π-π* transition of CC bonds [30]. Biomass CDs possessed the hydrophilic and hydrophobic functional groups, which made them stable with amphiphilic characteristics, thus facilitating their applications in various fields without any functionalization. When the maximum excitation wavelength of biomass CDs was appeared at 350 nm (Fig. S1A, line II), the maximum wavelength was at 456 nm (Fig. S1A, line III). When the excitation wavelength was ranging from 310 nm to 400 nm, the emission peak had a red shift (Fig. S1B). The phenomenon may be explained by synergistic effects of surface energy trap distribution and carbogenic core in biomass CDs [31]. In addition, the QY of CDs was 5.6%, which is comparable to that of other biomass CDs [32].

The fluorescence spectra of CDs dispersed in acetonitrile, DMF, ethanol, methanol, and water were also recorded to investigate the effect of solvation on fluorescence (Fig. S1C). The fluorescence intensity usually relies on the properties of solvent, including solvent viscosity, coordination ability, refractive index and so on [33]. The fluorescence intensity of CDs dispersed in polar aprotic solvents (acetonitrile and DMF) is higher than that dispersed in polar protic solvents (ethanol, methanol, and water). When CDs were dispersed in different solvents, there is hydrogen bonding interaction between CDs and solvents. Acetonitrile and DMF are polar aprotic solvents and cannot be hydrogen donors. While ethanol, methanol and water are polar protic solvents that can act as hydrogen donors. We speculate that hydrogen bonding between CDs and solvent can lead to the change of fluorescence intensity. The stronger the ability of solvents to supply hydrogen, the more defects on the surface of CDs are activated, causing the fluorescence intensity to decrease. Therefore, CDs fluorescence intensity in DMF was highest among them.

The morphology and size of biomass CDs and CDs@RAM-MIPs were studied. The TEM images (Fig. 2 A) showed that biomass CDs were spherical and well dispersed. The average diameter of biomass CDs was 2.8–4.2 nm (Fig. 2A insert), according to the particle size histogram. The particle size of CDs@RAM-MIPs (Fig. 2B) was much larger than that of CDs. The AFM image (Fig. S2A) indicated that particle heights were largely from 2 to 4 nm, which was consistent with the TEM results.

The broad peak of CDs@RAM-MIPs was located at 2θ = 20.3° and exhibited the amorphous nature of CDs (Fig. 2C) [34]. The thermal stability of CDs@RAM-MIPs composites was determined by TGA (Fig. S2B). The results showed that the initial weight loss of CDs@RAM-MIPs was attributed to the water molecules loss. The following weight loss may be attributed to the decomposition of some functional groups containing CO, C–H, O–H and C–O. The CDs@RAM-MIPs composites were totally decomposed at 453 °C. Meanwhile, the good thermal stability of CDs@RAM-MIPs composites was suitable for the practical requirement.

FT-IR analysis of biomass CDs, CDs@MIPs and CDs@RAM-MIPs was investigated to characterize the surface functional groups (Fig. 2D). For CDs, an obvious absorption peak at 3412 cm−1 belonged to the O–H stretching vibrations. The absorption peaks at 1419, 1720, 2931 cm−1 belonged to CC, CO and C–H stretching vibrations. This result revealed that biomass CDs had some –OH and –COOH functional groups, which made the surface of biomass CDs easy to interact with target molecules. The existence of CO groups may be the possible cause for long-range emission of CDs [35]. Furthermore, the synthesized CDs possessed good solubility because of the existence of hydrophobic (CC) and hydrophilic (CO) functional groups. These groups rendered amphiphilic property to biomass CDs. After preparing CDs@RAM-MIPs, the peaks at 3412, 2931, 1720, and 1419 cm−1 were shifted to 3447, 2985, 1727, and 1387 cm−1, respectively. Compared to CDs and CDs@MIPs, the peak of CDs@RAM-MIPs at 3447 cm−1 became wider, indicating that epoxide rings of CDs@RAM-MIPs had changed to –OH groups and the hydrophilic modification was successfully carried out [36]. The peaks of CDs@RAM-MIPs at 1158, 1453 cm−1 and 1560 cm−1 were related to C–O, CC and N–H, respectively.

The elemental analysis was also investigated in Fig. 3 A and B. The full-scale XPS analysis of biomass CDs had three peaks at 288.4, 402.8 and 535.6 eV, which belonged to C1s, N1s and O1s. The C1s spectrum had three peaks at 287, 288.2, 289.4 eV, which was assigned to C–C, C–O, CO, respectively. The two peaks of O1s spectrum at 534.6 and 535.7 eV were assigned to C–O and CO groups. The weak N1s peak was also given to confirm the existence of N elements. Besides, the C1s spectrum of CDs@RAM-MIPs can be deconvoluted into four peaks corresponding to C–C, C–O, CO and N–CO groups at 286.7, 289.8, 290.2, and 290.7 eV. The high-resolution O1s spectrum revealed two peaks at 534.1 and 535.1 eV, which were ascribed to the existence of C–O and CO. The two peaks of N1s spectrum at 400.8 and 402.2 eV corresponded to C–N and N–H groups. All peaks indicated that functionalized RAM-MIPs were generated by amide and ester bond.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Factors affecting the optical properties of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The photoluminescence properties of biomass CDs were influenced by some experimental conditions, including the influence of pH, salt concentration, etc. The CDs fluorescence intensity was investigated by different pH values (3-12) (Fig. S3A). The fluorescence intensity was increased from 3 to 5, then kept stable as pH ranging from 5 to 10, and sharply decreased at pH = 10–12. The result showed that biomass CDs had a wide range of pH adjustment.

The stability of biomass CDs was studied under different NaCl concentrations (0–500 mM) (Fig. S3B). There is no obvious fluorescence intensity change, revealing that biomass CDs had good stability at high ionic concentrations. The phenomenon indicated that biomass CDs can be applied in sensing applications under rigorous physiological conditions. Conventional fluorescent substances with photobleaching characteristics will cause fluorescence intensity to decrease under UV irradiation. The effect of UV irradiation on CDs was explored by measuring fluorescence intensity in each hour. As shown in Fig. S3C, UV irradiation hardly affected the fluorescence intensity of CDs, indicating that CDs had good photobleaching resistance. It is worth noting that CDs are relatively stable and can be stored for three months. The high stability of biomass CDs under different conditions is favorable for labeling and bio-sensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Factors affecting the optical properties of CDs@RAM-MIPs
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs@RAM-MIPs fluorescent intensity was measured as the excitation wavelength ranged. The maximum fluorescence signal was observed when CDs@RAM-MIPs were excited at 360 nm. After adding CDs@RAM-MIPs to MNZ solution, the influence of reaction time was studied (Fig. 4 A). The fluorescence intensity decreased rapidly and reached equilibrium in 2 min.

The effect of pH value was also investigated in Fig. 4B. Due to the protonation/deprotonation process, MNZ molecules have three ionic states at different pH values (Fig. 4C). The fluorescence-quenched efficiency increased in a pH range of 3–8, then reduced in a pH range of 8–12. The pKa value of MNZ was 3.03. When pH = 3–4, MNZ coexisted in two forms. As the pH increased, MNZ existed as amphoteric molecules. At acidic pH, the heteroatoms of MNZ or even recognition sites were protonated and the interaction between MNZ and CDs@RAM-MIPs was reduced. When pH = 8, the quenching effect was the best, which may due to the hydrogen bonding between CDs@RAM-MIPs and MNZ. At further pH (pH > 8), the quenching efficiency decreased. Herein, the experiment condition was set at pH = 8.

To investigate the fluorescence stability of CDs@RAM-MIPs, the composites were irradiated under UV light (353 nm) for 90 min. The fluorescence intensity of CDs@RAM-MIPs composites kept constant with a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 1.8% (Fig. 4D).

The hydrophobicity changes of composites were studied by static water contact angles. The static state contact angles of CDs@MIPs and CDs@RAM-MIPs were about 102.0° and 73.8°, respectively (Fig. 5 A). After grafting the RAM nanoparticles, the hydrophilicity of the fluorescent sensor based on MIPs is improved. We also investigated the dispersion stability of CDs@MIPs and CDs@RAM-MIPs after their dispersed mixtures being settled down for 0–30 min (Fig. 5B). Compared with CDs@MIPs composites, the CDs@RAM-MIPs had better dispersion stability. The phenomenon illustrated that the hydrophilicity was enhanced by epoxy modification.

To study the hydrophilic properties of CDs@RAM-MIPs, we conducted a protein exclusion test for BSA as shown in Table 1 . Polystyrene has the strongest hydrophobicity, the protein exclusion effect is the worst. And silica gel has the strongest hydrophilicity, so the protein exclusion rate is the highest. For CDs@MIPs, the exclusion rate of BSA is 77.2%. The protein exclusion rate of CDs@RAM-NIPs and CDs@RAM-MIPs are 94.2% and 95.5%, indicating that BSA is not attached to the surface of synthetic restricted composites. It proved that the surface of synthetic restricted material was rich in diol groups and had good hydrophilicity. The result further indicated that CDs@RAM-MIPs can achieve the ideal protein exclusion effect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of CDs@RAM-MIPs for MNZ detection
ce:section.ce:para: The mechanism of CDs@RAM-MIPs fluorescence quenching was studied. The UV absorption bands of MNZ, CDs@RAM-MIPs and CDs@RAM-MIPs with MNZ were obtained in Fig. 6 A. The UV peaks of MNZ are located at 229 nm and 320 nm, while the UV peak of CDs@RAM-MIPs is weak. In comparison with the UV absorption spectra of MNZ, the peaks of CDs@RAM-MIPs with MNZ have almost unchanged. The phenomenon illustrated that the fluorescence quenching process of CDs@RAM-MIPs with MNZ belonged to dynamic quenching. Moreover, no significant overlap was observed between the emission peak of CDs@RAM-MIPs and the UV peak of MNZ (Fig. 6A). It showed that fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) was excluded to explain the fluorescence quenching.

We supported a view that fluorescence quenching mechanism belonged to photoelectron transfer (PET). As shown in Fig. 6B, when photons excited molecules, the electrons of CDs can be excited from ground state to excited state. The excited electrons of CDs then returned to valence band to generate a fluorescent signal. Our study was carried out at pH = 8, when MNZ exists as amphoteric molecules. After adding MNZ to the system, the excited electrons of CDs can directly jump to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital of the complex, thus causing electron transfer and achieving the fluorescence quenching.

The thermodynamic experiment was tested at 293 K, 303 K and 313 K, respectively. The entropy change (ΔS
o ), enthalpy change (ΔH
o ) and free enthalpy change (ΔG
o ) were obtained by the following thermodynamic equations (2)–(4):
(2)
Δ
G
o
= - R T
ln
K
S V
(3)
Δ
G
o
= Δ
H
o
- Δ
S
o
T
(4)
ln
K
S V
=
Δ
S
o
R
-
Δ
H
o
R T
where T and R are the quenching temperature and universal gas constant (8.314 J mol−1 K−1). As we can see in Table S1, the quenching constant increased with the increasing temperature, indicating that the possible fluorescence quenching process belonged to dynamic quenching.

The ability of CDs@RAM-MIPs for the determination of MNZ was investigated. Fig. 7 displayed the effects of different MNZ concentrations on fluorescence of composites. The fluorescence intensity of CDs@RAM-MIPs decreased much more than that of CDs@RAM-NIPs at the same MNZ concentration. It revealed that CDs@RAM-MIPs had higher sensitivity and stronger binding capacity than CDs@RAM-NIPs, because of specific recognition sites for template molecules. The fluorescence quenching of CDs@RAM-MIPs and CDs@RAM-NIPs towards MNZ was examined by the following equation:
(5)
F
0
F
=
K
S V
C q + 1
Where F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensities in the absence and existence of MNZ. K
SV means the quenching constant, and Cq means the MNZ concentration. The fluorescence response F0/F showed a good linear relationship with MNZ concentrations (50–1200 ng mL−1). The regression equations were given: F0/F = 1.50 × 10−3 Cq+1.0432 (CDs@RAM-MIPs, R2 = 0.995), F0/F = 4.8 × 10−4 Cq+1.0380 (CDs@RAM-NIPs, R2 = 0.982). The limit of detection (LOD) was 17.4 ng mL−1 which was measured by 3σ/slope, where σ represents the deviation of blank measurements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity and selectivity for MNZ detection
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of CDs@RAM-MIPs was examined using other chemical structure analogues (DMZ and ONZ) as references, which was shown in Fig. S4. MNZ and its analogues can occupy the recognition cavities and result in fluorescence quenching of CDs@RAM-MIPs. The IF values of MNZ, ONZ and DMZ were 3.13, 2.13, 2.37, respectively (Table S2). CDs@RAM-MIPs have the highest affinity for template MNZ. This is because the surface of CDs@RAM-MIPs can generate a large number of specific binding sites for MNZ. Compared with other structure analogues, these cavities are similar to MNZ in spatial arrangement size and shape. We also used cloramphenicol as a reference substance to study the recognition ability of CDs@RAM-MIPs (Fig. S4D). The IF of chloramphenicol was 1.15 (Table S2), revealing that CDs@RAM-MIPs had no specific imprint sites for chloramphenicol.

Usually, there are some interfering compounds in serum samples. In order to investigate whether coexisting substances have impact on MNZ detection, further interference tests were carried out (Table S3). The results showed that coexisting substances have no significant influence on MNZ detection, because of high selectivity of CDs@RAM-MIPs. In general, CDs@RAM-MIPs provide more particular recognition cavities to selectively bind MNZ. The established CDs@RAM-MIPs method offers new developments for detecting target compounds in complex samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Practical application in analyzing serum sample
ce:section.ce:para: The applicability of reported approach was studied by detecting the content of MNZ in serum samples. No MNZ at detectable level are discovered in these samples. Different levels of MNZ concentrations (50, 500 and 1000 ng mL−1) were added to serum samples and detected. Recoveries of MNZ ranged from 93.5% to 102.7% and RSDs were 1.9%–3.6%. In addition, CDs@MIPs were used as comparative materials to detect MNZ in serum, the recoveries of MNZ was 80.3%–89.4%, respectively. The results revealed that CDs@RAM-MIPs composites were applicable to detect MNZ in real serum samples.

Besides, the analytical performance of some methods such as liquid chromatographic [4], electrochemical method [5], and fluorescence spectrophotometer analysis [37] was compared for MNZ detection (Table 2 ). The LODs, recoveries and RSDs of the proposed approach were comparable to other detection methods. Different approaches have different merits and drawbacks in terms of specific recognition, accuracy and analysis time. In comparison with other reported detection methods, our method has several advantages: (1) The combination of RAM and molecular imprinting technologies provides dual identification element to improve the anti-interference ability and selectivity. (2) Compared with liquid chromatography methods, fluorescence analysis methods have the merits of no complicated pretreatment process, simplicity, and rapidity. Thus, CDs@RAM-MIPs fluorescent sensor has good properties for selective detection of MNZ.

An effective and sensitive fluorescence strategy was established to detect MNZ using CDs@RAM-MIPs system. The biomass CDs were prepared using longan peels by high pressure microwave method, and their applications have solved the problems of toxicity and high cost. Furthermore, combining RAM-MIPs with CDs can improve the selectivity and sensitivity of fluorescence probe. A possible mechanism was proposed that dynamic quenching and electron transfer would cause the fluorescence quenching. We introduced the RAM technique to provide hydrophilic modification for the composites, which is more suitable for biological media. The increased water compatibility and the ability of excluding biological macromolecules were confirmed by the change of contact angles and protein binding experiments. This platform has sensitively and selectively detected MNZ in serum samples. However, the limitation of this method is that the synthesis process of CDs@RAM-MIPs needs relatively long time. This strategy was expected to provide a novel fluorescent sensor to detect trace substance in biological applications.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51778175), the State Key Laboratory of Urban Water Resource and Environment (Harbin Institute of Technology) (NO. 2018DX06) and the Provincial Nature Science Foundation of Heilongjiang (No. E2016039).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2019.120508.
